papers.title: BadNets: Backdoor Attacks on Deep Neural Networks
papers.tldr: backdoor attack by poisoning training data with a trigger
papers.abstract: We introduce backdoor attacks in which an adversary poisons a fraction of the training set with a small trigger pattern, so the trained network misclassifies any input containing the trigger while behaving normally on clean inputs.

papers.title: Trojaning Attack on Neural Networks
papers.tldr: implant a trojan trigger into a trained network
papers.abstract: We show how to implant a hidden trigger into an already trained neural network so that inputs carrying the trigger yield an attacker chosen output, without access to the original training data.

papers.title: Poison Frogs: Targeted Clean-Label Poisoning Attacks
papers.tldr: clean-label data poisoning targeting a specific input
papers.abstract: We craft clean-label poisoned training examples that look correctly labelled yet cause a specific target test instance to be misclassified after the victim retrains on the poisoned set.

papers.title: Explaining and Harnessing Adversarial Examples
papers.tldr: fast gradient sign method generates adversarial examples
papers.abstract: We argue that neural networks are vulnerable to adversarial examples because of their linear nature, and introduce the fast gradient sign method to craft small perturbations that cause confident misclassification.

papers.title: Towards Deep Learning Models Resistant to Adversarial Attacks
papers.tldr: projected gradient descent adversarial training for robustness
papers.abstract: We study adversarial robustness through robust optimization, using projected gradient descent to find worst-case perturbations and adversarial training to produce networks resistant to such attacks.

papers.title: Adversarial Patch
papers.tldr: physical adversarial patch fools image classifiers
papers.abstract: We construct a universal adversarial patch that, when placed anywhere in a scene, causes classifiers to report a chosen target class regardless of the surrounding image content.

papers.title: Deep Learning with Differential Privacy
papers.tldr: DP-SGD clips gradients and adds noise for privacy
papers.abstract: We train neural networks under differential privacy by clipping per-example gradients and adding calibrated Gaussian noise, and introduce a moments accountant that gives tight bounds on the cumulative privacy loss.

papers.title: Semi-supervised Knowledge Transfer with PATE
papers.tldr: private aggregation of teacher ensembles for privacy
papers.abstract: Private aggregation of teacher ensembles trains many teachers on disjoint partitions of sensitive data and transfers their aggregated, noised votes to a student model with formal differential privacy guarantees.

papers.title: Renyi Differential Privacy
papers.tldr: Renyi divergence relaxation with tighter composition
papers.abstract: We propose a relaxation of differential privacy based on the Renyi divergence that supports tighter and simpler composition of the privacy cost across a long sequence of mechanisms.

papers.title: Semi-Supervised Classification with Graph Convolutional Networks
papers.tldr: graph convolutional network for node classification
papers.abstract: We present a graph convolutional network that performs semi-supervised node classification by aggregating feature information from neighboring nodes through spectral graph convolutions.

papers.title: Inductive Representation Learning on Large Graphs (GraphSAGE)
papers.tldr: inductive node embeddings by sampling and aggregating neighbors
papers.abstract: GraphSAGE learns inductive node representations on large graphs by sampling and aggregating features from a node's local neighborhood, generalizing to nodes and graphs unseen during training.

papers.title: Graph Attention Networks
papers.tldr: attention over neighbors for graph node classification
papers.abstract: Graph attention networks assign learnable attention coefficients to a node's neighbors when aggregating their features, improving node classification over graph-structured relational data.

queries.q: backdoor attack poisoning training data with a trigger
making a network respond to a secret hidden pattern planted during training
adversarial examples perturbations fooling classifiers robustness
tiny imperceptible input changes that make a model predict the wrong class
differential privacy deep learning gradient noise
provable guarantees that a model does not leak sensitive user records
graph neural network node classification neighbor aggregation
learning embeddings over relational data organized as nodes and links
